title: Longest Substring With At Most K Distinct
description: Find max substring with at most k distinct chars

examples.explanation: String length n=5. Initialize max_len=0. Check all substrings: i=0,j=0: substring="e", set={'e'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len=1. i=0,j=1: substring="ec", set={'e','c'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len=2. i=0,j=2: substring="ece", set={'e','c'}, distinct=2≤2, length=3, max_len=3. i=0,j=3: substring="eceb", set={'e','c','b'}, distinct=3>2, skip. i=0,j=4: substring="eceba", set={'e','c','b','a'}, distinct=4>2, skip. i=1,j=1: substring="c", set={'c'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. i=1,j=2: substring="ce", set={'c','e'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len stays 3. i=1,j=3: substring="ceb", set={'c','e','b'}, distinct=3>2, skip. i=1,j=4: substring="ceba", set={'c','e','b','a'}, distinct=4>2, skip. i=2,j=2: substring="e", set={'e'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. i=2,j=3: substring="eb", set={'e','b'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len stays 3. i=2,j=4: substring="eba", set={'e','b','a'}, distinct=3>2, skip. i=3,j=3: substring="b", set={'b'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. i=3,j=4: substring="ba", set={'b','a'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len stays 3. i=4,j=4: substring="a", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. Final max_len=3.

String length n=2. Initialize max_len=0. Check substrings: i=0,j=0: substring="a", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤1, length=1, max_len=1. i=0,j=1: substring="aa", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤1, length=2, max_len=2. i=1,j=1: substring="a", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤1, length=1, max_len stays 2. Final max_len=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Check all possible substrings by iterating over start and end indices, and for each substring, count distinct characters using a set or array. Use this to understand the problem but avoid in practice due to inefficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n³) Solution for Longest Substring With At Most K Distinct Characters
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all possible substrings and counting distinct characters for each.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by systematically examining every possible substring of the input string. For each substring defined by start index i and end index j, we need to determine if it contains at most k distinct characters. We achieve this by iterating through all characters in the substring and using a set to track unique characters. The size of this set gives us the distinct character count. If this count is ≤ k, we compare the substring length (j-i+1) with our current maximum length, updating if longer. The intuition is exhaustive search: by checking every substring, we guarantee finding the optimal solution. The algorithm uses three nested loops: outer loop for start index, middle loop for end index, and inner loop to examine characters in the substring. Time complexity is O(n³) because for each of O(n²) substrings, we may examine up to O(n) characters. Space complexity is O(min(n, k)) since the set stores at most min(n, k) characters. This connects to L1 by using the distinct character counting subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in substring indices, forgetting to reset the set for each substring, and inefficiently recounting characters. Real code pattern: initialize max_len=0; for i from 0 to n-1: for j from i to n-1: create empty set; for char in s[i:j+1]: add to set; if len(set) ≤ k: max_len = max(max_len, j-i+1); return max_len.

examples.trace: String length n=5. Initialize max_len=0. Check all substrings: i=0,j=0: substring="e", set={'e'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len=1. i=0,j=1: substring="ec", set={'e','c'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len=2. i=0,j=2: substring="ece", set={'e','c'}, distinct=2≤2, length=3, max_len=3. i=0,j=3: substring="eceb", set={'e','c','b'}, distinct=3>2, skip. i=0,j=4: substring="eceba", set={'e','c','b','a'}, distinct=4>2, skip. i=1,j=1: substring="c", set={'c'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. i=1,j=2: substring="ce", set={'c','e'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len stays 3. i=1,j=3: substring="ceb", set={'c','e','b'}, distinct=3>2, skip. i=1,j=4: substring="ceba", set={'c','e','b','a'}, distinct=4>2, skip. i=2,j=2: substring="e", set={'e'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. i=2,j=3: substring="eb", set={'e','b'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len stays 3. i=2,j=4: substring="eba", set={'e','b','a'}, distinct=3>2, skip. i=3,j=3: substring="b", set={'b'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. i=3,j=4: substring="ba", set={'b','a'}, distinct=2≤2, length=2, max_len stays 3. i=4,j=4: substring="a", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤2, length=1, max_len stays 3. Final max_len=3.

String length n=2. Initialize max_len=0. Check substrings: i=0,j=0: substring="a", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤1, length=1, max_len=1. i=0,j=1: substring="aa", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤1, length=2, max_len=2. i=1,j=1: substring="a", set={'a'}, distinct=1≤1, length=1, max_len stays 2. Final max_len=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Counting Distinct Characters in a Substring
ladders.desc: Given a substring, determine the number of distinct characters it contains.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach. Given a substring defined by start and end indices in a string, we need to count how many unique characters appear within that range. The intuition is to use a set data structure, which automatically handles uniqueness: as we iterate through each character in the substring, we add it to the set. After processing all characters, the size of the set gives the distinct count. Alternatively, we could use a frequency array of size 256 for ASCII characters, but a set is more general. The algorithm: initialize an empty set; loop through each character from start to end inclusive; add character to set; after loop, return len(set). Time complexity is O(m) where m is substring length, as each character is processed once. Space complexity is O(min(m, k)) since the set stores unique characters. This feeds into L0 by being called for every substring. Common mistakes include incorrect loop boundaries (off-by-one), not resetting the set for each new substring, and using inefficient data structures. Real code pattern: function count_distinct(s, start, end): distinct_set = set(); for i in range(start, end+1): distinct_set.add(s[i]); return len(distinct_set).

examples.trace: Substring from index 1 to 3 inclusive: s[1]='e', s[2]='l', s[3]='l'. Initialize set={}. Process 'e': set={'e'}. Process 'l': set={'e','l'}. Process 'l': 'l' already in set, set stays {'e','l'}. Final set size=2.

Substring from index 2 to 5 inclusive: s[2]='b', s[3]='b', s[4]='a', s[5]='a'. Initialize set={}. Process s[2]='b': set={'b'}. Process s[3]='b': 'b' already in set, set stays {'b'}. Process s[4]='a': set={'b','a'}. Process s[5]='a': 'a' already in set, set stays {'b','a'}. Final set size=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over All Possible Substrings
ladders.desc: Generate every substring of a string by varying start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique in the brute force approach: to examine all substrings, we systematically vary the start index i from 0 to n-1 and for each i, vary the end index j from i to n-1. This produces all possible substrings, as any substring can be uniquely identified by its start and end indices. The intuition is combinatorial: there are n choices for start and up to n choices for end, giving O(n²) substrings. The algorithm uses two nested loops: outer loop for i, inner loop for j. For each pair (i,j), we define substring s[i:j+1]. This feeds into L1 by providing the substring to analyze. Time complexity is O(n²) for generating substrings, but in the full problem, each substring processing adds O(n) time. Space complexity is O(1) for indices. Common mistakes include incorrect loop ranges (e.g., j from 0 instead of i), misunderstanding substring inclusivity, and not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: n = len(s); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substring = s[i:j+1]; then process substring.

examples.trace: String length n=3. i=0: j=0 → substring s[0:1]="a"; j=1 → s[0:2]="ab"; j=2 → s[0:3]="abc". i=1: j=1 → s[1:2]="b"; j=2 → s[1:3]="bc". i=2: j=2 → s[2:3]="c". Total 6 substrings.
String length n=2. i=0: j=0 → substring s[0:1]="x"; j=1 → s[0:2]="xy". i=1: j=1 → s[1:2]="y". Total 3 substrings.

ladders.title: Building Block — Using Sets to Track Unique Elements
ladders.desc: Maintain a collection of unique items using a set data structure.
ladders.explanation: A set is a fundamental data structure that stores unique elements. In this context, we use it to track distinct characters in a substring. The intuition is that sets automatically enforce uniqueness: when you add an element, if it already exists, the set remains unchanged. This makes counting distinct items efficient. The algorithm involves initializing an empty set, then iterating through items and adding each to the set. After processing, the size of the set gives the count of unique items. Time complexity for adding m items is O(m) on average, as each insertion is O(1). Space complexity is O(u) where u is number of unique items. This feeds into L2 by being the method to count distinct characters for each substring. Common mistakes include confusing sets with lists (which allow duplicates), not importing set module if needed, and assuming order in sets. Real code pattern: unique_set = set(); for item in collection: unique_set.add(item); count = len(unique_set).

examples.trace: Initialize set={}. Add 'a': set={'a'}. Add 'b': set={'a','b'}. Add 'a': 'a' already in set, set stays {'a','b'}. Add 'c': set={'a','b','c'}. Add 'b': 'b' already in set, set stays {'a','b','c'}. Final set size=3.

Initialize set={}. Add 1: set={1}. Add 2: set={1,2}. Add 2: 2 already in set, set stays {1,2}. Add 3: set={1,2,3}. Add 3: 3 already in set, set stays {1,2,3}. Add 3: 3 already in set, set stays {1,2,3}. Final set size=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through String Characters with Indices
ladders.desc: Access each character in a string using index-based loops.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves traversing a string character by character using indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences, and we can access individual characters via integer indices starting from 0. The algorithm uses a loop with an index variable that goes from 0 to len(string)-1, and at each step, we access string[index]. This is fundamental for processing substrings. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing entire string. Space complexity is O(1) for the index. This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to feed characters into the set. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, off-by-one in loop conditions, and confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)): char = s[i]; then process char.

examples.output: Characters: h, e, l, l, o

examples.trace: String length=2. i=0: s[0]='a'. i=1: s[1]='b'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Sequences of Characters
ladders.desc: A string is an ordered collection of characters that can be accessed by position.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a string is a data type representing a sequence of characters. Each character has a position or index, starting from 0. For example, in string "cat", index 0 is 'c', index 1 is 'a', index 2 is 't'. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains. Strings are immutable in many languages, meaning you cannot change individual characters directly. The intuition is that strings behave like arrays of characters, allowing access via indices. This feeds into L4 by enabling character iteration. Common mistakes include treating strings as mutable, confusing indices (e.g., thinking index starts at 1), and not accounting for empty strings. Real code pattern: s = "example"; length = len(s); first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[length-1].

examples.trace: String "dog" has 3 characters. Position 0: character 'd'. Position 1: character 'o'. Position 2: character 'g'. Length is 3.
Empty string has no characters. Length is 0. There are no indices to access.

approaches.name: Sliding Window with HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a sliding window using two pointers and a hashmap to track character frequencies. Expand the right pointer to add characters, and shrink the left pointer when distinct characters exceed k. This efficiently finds the maximum length in linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Substring With At Most K Distinct Characters
ladders.desc: Complete sliding window solution using hashmap to track character frequencies
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the longest substring containing at most k distinct characters. The sliding window approach with a hashmap provides an optimal O(n) solution. We maintain a window defined by left and right pointers, expanding the right pointer to add characters and shrinking the left pointer when the number of distinct characters exceeds k. The hashmap tracks character frequencies within the current window, allowing us to efficiently determine when to shrink the window. The intuition is that we want to explore all possible substrings while maintaining the constraint of at most k distinct characters. By sliding the window, we can efficiently find the maximum length without checking every substring explicitly. The algorithm initializes left pointer at 0, an empty hashmap, and max_length at 0. As we iterate through the string with the right pointer, we add the current character to the hashmap (incrementing its count). If the hashmap size exceeds k, we shrink the window from the left: decrement the count of the character at left pointer, and if it reaches 0, remove it from the hashmap, then increment left pointer. After each step, we update max_length with the current window size (right-left+1). Time complexity is O(n) since each character is processed at most twice (once by right pointer, once by left). Space complexity is O(k) for the hashmap storing at most k+1 distinct characters. This approach efficiently finds the solution by dynamically adjusting the window to maintain the constraint while exploring all possibilities.

examples.trace:
Initialize: left=0, freq={}, max_len=0
Right=0: char='e', freq={'e':1}, distinct=1 ≤ 2, window_size=1, max_len=1
Right=1: char='c', freq={'e':1,'c':1}, distinct=2 ≤ 2, window_size=2, max_len=2
Right=2: char='e', freq={'e':2,'c':1}, distinct=2 ≤ 2, window_size=3, max_len=3
Right=3: char='b', freq={'e':2,'c':1,'b':1}, distinct=3 > 2 → shrink: remove 'e' (left=0), freq={'e':1,'c':1,'b':1}, distinct=3 > 2 → remove 'c' (left=1), freq={'e':1,'b':1}, distinct=2 ≤ 2, left=2, window_size=2
Right=4: char='a', freq={'e':1,'b':1,'a':1}, distinct=3 > 2 → shrink: remove 'e' (left=2), freq={'b':1,'a':1}, distinct=2 ≤ 2, left=3, window_size=2
Final max_len=3.

Initialize: left=0, freq={}, max_len=0
Right=0: char='a', freq={'a':1}, distinct=1 ≤ k=1, window_size=1, max_len=1
Right=1: char='a', freq={'a':2}, distinct=1 ≤ 1, window_size=2, max_len=2
Final max_len=2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Window Shrinking Logic
ladders.desc: Shrinking the window from left when distinct characters exceed k
ladders.explanation: The window shrinking subroutine is crucial for maintaining the constraint of at most k distinct characters. When the hashmap size exceeds k, we need to reduce the window from the left until we're back within the limit. The intuition is that by moving the left pointer rightward, we remove characters from the window, decreasing their frequencies in the hashmap. When a character's frequency drops to 0, we remove it from the hashmap, reducing the distinct count. This ensures the window always satisfies the constraint while allowing us to continue expanding with the right pointer. The algorithm: while hashmap size > k: get character at left pointer, decrement its count in hashmap, if count becomes 0, remove it from hashmap, increment left pointer. This process continues until hashmap size ≤ k. Time complexity is O(n) amortized since each character is removed at most once. Space complexity is O(k) for the hashmap. This subroutine connects to the full problem by enabling the sliding window to dynamically adjust, ensuring we explore valid substrings efficiently. Common mistakes include not removing characters when count reaches 0 (leading to incorrect distinct count) or shrinking too much (breaking the window). The real code pattern uses a while loop and careful hashmap updates.

examples.trace:
Initial: freq size=3 > k=2
Step 1: char at left=0 is 'a', decrement freq['a'] to 1, freq={'a':1,'b':1,'c':1}, size=3 > 2, left=1
Step 2: char at left=1 is 'a', decrement freq['a'] to 0, remove 'a', freq={'b':1,'c':1}, size=2 ≤ 2, left=2
Final: left=2, freq={'b':1,'c':1}

Initial: freq size=1 ≤ k=1, no shrinking needed
Final: left=0, freq={'x':1}

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Expansion
ladders.desc: Expanding the window by moving right pointer and updating hashmap
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the sliding window approach involves expanding the window to include new characters while tracking their frequencies. We start with left=0 and move right from 0 to n-1. For each position, we add the character at right pointer to the hashmap: if it exists, increment its count; if not, add it with count 1. This expansion allows us to explore longer substrings. The intuition is that by systematically increasing the window size, we can find all possible substrings that might be valid. After adding the character, we check if the hashmap size (number of distinct characters) exceeds k. If it does, we trigger the shrinking subroutine (level 1); otherwise, we update the maximum length. The expansion ensures we consider every character as a potential end of a valid substring. Time complexity for expansion is O(n) as each character is added once. Space complexity is O(k) for the hashmap. This connects to the full problem by providing the mechanism to grow the window and discover new substrings. Common mistakes include updating max_length before shrinking (should be after ensuring constraint is met) or not handling empty strings. The real code pattern uses a for loop for right pointer and direct hashmap updates.

examples.input: s="abc", k=2, start with left=0, freq={}, max_len=0
examples.trace:
Right=0: char='a', freq={'a':1}, distinct=1 ≤ 2, window_size=1, max_len=1
Right=1: char='b', freq={'a':1,'b':1}, distinct=2 ≤ 2, window_size=2, max_len=2
Right=2: char='c', freq={'a':1,'b':1,'c':1}, distinct=3 > 2 → shrink (not detailed here), after shrinking max_len remains 2
Final max_len=2

examples.input: s="xx", k=1, start with left=0, freq={}, max_len=0
examples.trace:
Right=0: char='x', freq={'x':1}, distinct=1 ≤ 1, window_size=1, max_len=1
Right=1: char='x', freq={'x':2}, distinct=1 ≤ 1, window_size=2, max_len=2
Final max_len=2

ladders.title: Building Block — Character Frequency Tracking with HashMap
ladders.desc: Using a hashmap to maintain counts of characters in current window
ladders.explanation: Tracking character frequencies within the sliding window is essential for determining the number of distinct characters. A hashmap (or dictionary) allows O(1) average-time insertions, updates, and lookups. The intuition is that by storing each character as a key and its count as value, we can quickly add new characters, increment counts, and remove characters when their count drops to 0. This enables efficient window management. The algorithm involves: when adding a character at right pointer, check if it exists in hashmap; if yes, increment its count; if no, add it with count 1. When removing a character during shrinking, decrement its count; if count becomes 0, remove the key entirely. The hashmap size gives the number of distinct characters instantly. Time complexity for each operation is O(1) amortized. Space complexity is O(k) for storing up to k+1 distinct characters. This building block connects to the core logic by providing the data structure needed for expansion and shrinking. Common mistakes include not removing keys when count reaches 0 (leading to inflated distinct count) or using inefficient data structures. The real code pattern uses a dictionary with get() or defaultdict.

examples.input: Add characters: 'a','b','a' to empty freq map

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Two Pointer Movement
ladders.desc: Incrementing left and right pointers to define window boundaries
ladders.explanation: The basic operation of moving two pointers (left and right) defines the sliding window. The right pointer expands the window to include new characters, while the left pointer shrinks it to maintain constraints. The intuition is that by adjusting these pointers, we can represent any substring of the original string. The algorithm initializes left=0, then iterates right from 0 to n-1. At each step, the window is s[left:right+1]. When we need to shrink, we increment left until the constraint is satisfied. Pointer movement is fundamental because it allows us to traverse the string efficiently without generating all substrings explicitly. Time complexity is O(n) as each pointer moves at most n times. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointers. This operation connects to the building block by providing the indices to access characters for frequency tracking. Common mistakes include moving pointers incorrectly (e.g., left > right) or not updating them in sync with the hashmap. The real code pattern uses integer variables and simple increments.

examples.input: Move right from 0 to 2, then left from 0 to 1
examples.trace:
Start: left=0, right=0, window="s[0:1]"
Step 1: right=1, window="s[0:2]"
Step 2: right=2, window="s[0:3]"
Step 3: left=1, window="s[1:3]"
Final: left=1, right=2, window="s[1:3]"

examples.input: Move right from 0 to 0, left stays 0
examples.trace:
Start: left=0, right=0, window="s[0:1]"
No further moves
Final: left=0, right=0, window="s[0:1]"

testCases.input: left=0, right=2, move right to 3 and left to 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Substrings and Distinct Characters
ladders.desc: Understanding substrings and counting distinct characters in a string
ladders.explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For example, in "abc", substrings include "a", "ab", "abc", "b", "bc", "c". Distinct characters are unique characters ignoring duplicates; e.g., in "aab", distinct characters are {'a','b'} (count 2). The intuition is that to solve the problem, we need to examine substrings and check how many different characters they contain. This foundation is crucial because the problem asks for the longest substring with at most k distinct characters. We can find all substrings by considering start and end indices, but that's inefficient (O(n²)). Instead, we'll use a sliding window for efficiency. Counting distinct characters can be done by iterating through the substring and using a set, but we'll optimize with a hashmap. Time complexity for naive substring generation and distinct counting is O(n³) if done separately. Space complexity for storing distinct characters is O(n). This connects to basic pointer movement by defining what we're measuring. Common mistakes include confusing substrings with subsequences (which don't require contiguity) or miscounting distinct characters. The real code pattern involves loops and sets for simple cases.

examples.input: String="apple", find all substrings and distinct counts for k=3
examples.output: Longest substring with ≤3 distinct chars is "apple" (length 5)
examples.trace:
Substrings:
"a": distinct={'a'}, count=1 ≤3, length=1
"ap": distinct={'a','p'}, count=2 ≤3, length=2
"app": distinct={'a','p'}, count=2 ≤3, length=3
"appl": distinct={'a','p','l'}, count=3 ≤3, length=4
"apple": distinct={'a','p','l','e'}, count=4 >3
"p": distinct={'p'}, count=1 ≤3, length=1
"pp": distinct={'p'}, count=1 ≤3, length=2
... continue, longest is "appl" with length 4, but actually "apple" has 4 distinct >3, so check: "appl" length 4, "apple" invalid, so max is 4

approaches.name: Sliding Window with Ordered Map O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Use a sliding window with an ordered map (e.g., TreeMap in Java) to store the last occurrence index of each character. When distinct characters exceed k, move the left pointer to the minimum last occurrence plus one. This approach handles character removal more precisely.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Substring With At Most K Distinct Characters
ladders.desc: Complete solution using sliding window with ordered map to track last occurrences
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires finding the longest substring containing at most k distinct characters. The sliding window approach with an ordered map provides an optimal O(n log k) solution. The intuition is to maintain a window [left, right] that always satisfies the k-distinct constraint. We use an ordered map (like TreeMap in Java or sorted dictionary in Python) to store the last occurrence index of each character currently in the window. When we exceed k distinct characters, we need to remove the character with the smallest last occurrence index, as this allows us to shrink the window minimally while maintaining the constraint. The algorithm proceeds by expanding the right pointer through the string, updating the last occurrence of each character in the map. When distinct characters exceed k, we find the character with the minimum last occurrence index, move left to that index + 1, and remove that character from the map. At each step, we update the maximum window length. The ordered map enables O(log k) operations for insertion, deletion, and finding the minimum last occurrence. Time complexity is O(n log k) where n is string length, as each character is processed once with map operations taking O(log k). Space complexity is O(k) for storing up to k+1 character mappings. This approach is more precise than simple frequency counting because it allows us to remove exactly the character that will shrink the window the least, maximizing the remaining window size. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings, incorrectly updating left pointer, or using inefficient data structures that degrade performance. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the key subroutine of finding and removing the character with minimum last occurrence when the window exceeds k distinct characters.

Pseudocode:
function longestSubstring(s, k):
    if k == 0 or len(s) == 0: return 0
    left = 0
    max_len = 0
    last_occurrence = OrderedMap()  # char -> last index
    
    for right in range(len(s)):
        # Add current character to map
        last_occurrence[s[right]] = right
        
        # If we have more than k distinct chars
        while len(last_occurrence) > k:
            # Find char with smallest last occurrence
            min_char = last_occurrence.first_key()  # O(1) for ordered map
            min_index = last_occurrence[min_char]
            
            # Move left pointer past this character
            left = min_index + 1
            
            # Remove this character from map
            last_occurrence.delete(min_char)
        
        # Update max length
        max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
    
    return max_len

examples.trace:
Initialize: left=0, max_len=0, map={}
Right=0: char='e', map={'e':0}, len=1≤2, max_len=1
Right=1: char='c', map={'e':0,'c':1}, len=2≤2, max_len=2
Right=2: char='e', map={'e':2,'c':1}, len=2≤2, max_len=3
Right=3: char='b', map={'e':2,'c':1,'b':3}, len=3>2 → find min last occurrence: 'c' at index 1, left=1+1=2, remove 'c', map={'e':2,'b':3}, max_len=3
Right=4: char='a', map={'e':2,'b':3,'a':4}, len=3>2 → find min: 'e' at index 2, left=2+1=3, remove 'e', map={'b':3,'a':4}, max_len=3
Result: 3.

Initialize: left=0, max_len=0, map={}
right=0 (char='a'): map={'a':0}, len=1≤1, max_len=max(0,0-0+1)=1
right=1 (char='a'): map={'a':1}, len=1≤1, max_len=max(1,1-0+1)=2
Result: 2 (substring "aa")

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Remove Character with Minimum Last Occurrence
ladders.desc: When window exceeds k distinct chars, find char with smallest last index, move left pointer, and remove it
ladders.explanation:
This is the critical subroutine that maintains the sliding window constraint. When our window contains more than k distinct characters, we need to remove one character to satisfy the condition. The optimal choice is to remove the character whose last occurrence is earliest (smallest index), because this allows us to move the left pointer as little as possible while still reducing the distinct character count. The intuition is that by removing the character that appears furthest to the left in our current window, we eliminate it completely from the window (since we move left past its last occurrence), and we minimize the reduction in window size. The algorithm: 1) From the ordered map of character→last_index, find the character with the minimum last_index value. 2) Calculate new left pointer position as min_index + 1. 3) Remove this character from the map. This operation takes O(log k) time with an ordered map (O(k) with unordered map). The connection to L0 is that this subroutine is called whenever len(map) > k in the main loop. Common mistakes include removing the wrong character (e.g., removing based on first occurrence instead of last occurrence), not updating left pointer correctly, or using linear search instead of ordered map operations. This subroutine ensures the window remains valid while maximizing its size.

Pseudocode:
function removeMinLastOccurrence(map, left):
    # map is ordered by value (last occurrence index)
    min_char = map.first_key()  # Character with smallest last index
    min_index = map[min_char]
    
    # Update left pointer to exclude this character
    new_left = min_index + 1
    
    # Remove this character from map
    map.delete(min_char)
    
    return new_left

examples.trace: Current map has 3 entries (>k=2). Find min last occurrence: 'a' at index2 (smallest of 2,5,3). New left = 2+1 = 3. Remove 'a' from map. Result: map now {'b':5, 'c':3}, left updated to 3.
Map has 3 entries (>k=2). Find min: 'y' at index4 (smallest of 7,4,9). New left = 4+1 = 5. Remove 'y'. Result: map={'x':7, 'z':9}, left=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining K Distinct Constraint with Last Occurrence Tracking
ladders.desc: Using last occurrence indices to know exactly when characters leave the window
ladders.explanation:
The core insight of this approach is tracking the last occurrence index of each character in the current window, rather than just counting frequencies. This allows precise window adjustment when we exceed k distinct characters. The intuition: if we only track frequencies, when we need to remove a character, we don't know how far to move the left pointer. By tracking last occurrences, we know exactly which character will exit the window first if we move left past a certain point. The algorithm maintains an ordered map from character to its last seen index within [left, right]. When adding a new character at position right, we update its last occurrence to right. When len(map) > k, we find the character with minimum last occurrence index - this is the character that will completely exit the window if we move left past that index. We then move left to min_index + 1, removing that character from the map. This ensures the window always contains at most k distinct characters. Time complexity for maintaining this constraint is O(log k) per operation with an ordered map. The connection to L1 is that this logic is implemented by the key subroutine. Common mistakes include confusing last occurrence with first occurrence, not handling duplicate characters correctly, or using inefficient data structures. This approach works because it guarantees we remove exactly one distinct character when needed, and we remove the one that allows us to keep the window as large as possible.

Pseudocode:
function maintainConstraint(map, char, right, k, left):
    # Update last occurrence of current character
    map[char] = right
    
    # Check if we exceed k distinct characters
    if len(map) > k:
        # Find character with minimum last occurrence
        min_char = None
        min_index = infinity
        for ch, idx in map.items():
            if idx < min_index:
                min_index = idx
                min_char = ch
        
        # Move left pointer past this character
        left = min_index + 1
        
        # Remove this character from map
        map.delete(min_char)
    
    return left

examples.trace: Add 'c': map={'a':1,'b':3,'c':4}. len=3>k=2. Find min: 'a' at index1. left=1+1=2. Remove 'a'. Result: map={'b':3,'c':4}, left=2.
Update 'x': map={'x':7}. len=1≤k=1. No removal needed. left unchanged at 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Ordered Map Operations for Character Tracking
ladders.desc: Using sorted data structure to maintain character→last_index mappings with efficient min/max operations

examples.input: Operations: put('a',2), put('b',5), put('c',3), get_min(), delete('a'), get_min()
examples.output: After puts: min=(2,'a'). After delete: min=(3,'c')

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Updating Last Occurrence of a Character in Collection
ladders.desc: Insert or update the last seen position for a character in a mapping
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves maintaining a mapping from characters to their most recent (last) occurrence index. When we encounter a character at position i, we need to update its entry in our data structure to value i. If the character is not already in the map, we insert it with value i. If it already exists, we update its value to i (since this is now the last occurrence). The intuition is simple: we always want to know the rightmost position where each character appears within our current window. This information is crucial for determining when a character completely exits the window when we move the left pointer. The algorithm is straightforward: check if character exists in map; if yes, update its value to current index; if no, insert new entry with current index. Time complexity is O(1) for hash map or O(log n) for ordered map. Space complexity is O(1) per update. Connection to L3: this operation is used repeatedly in the ordered map. Common mistakes: forgetting to update when character already exists (leading to incorrect last occurrence), or confusing last occurrence with first occurrence. This operation must handle both insert and update cases correctly.

Pseudocode:
function updateLastOccurrence(map, char, index):
    # Insert or update the last occurrence of char
    map[char] = index
    # That's it - assignment handles both insert and update

examples.trace: Character 'c' not in map. Insert new entry: map['c'] = 3. Result: map has 3 entries.
Character 'x' exists in map with value 5. Update: map['x'] = 7. Value changes from 5 to 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Counting Distinct Characters in a String
ladders.desc: Determining how many different characters appear in a given string or substring
